course_description:
COMU7311 provides specialised, advanced, and integrated knowledge and skills for research and professional practice in intercultural communication. Students will critically review a complex body of knowledge in intercultural communication, reflect on and synthesise theories and research, apply and transmit knowledge and skills to specialist and non-specialist audiences. The general goal of this course is to equip students with the capacity to demonstrate autonomy, expert judgement, adaptability and responsibility as effective communicators in intercultural contexts.

COMU7311 equips you with knowledge of intercultural communication and skills for applying this knowledge in professional practice. The course critically examines how culture influences ﾠperception and behaviour, what role communication plays in developing intercultural relations andﾠresolving conflicts, and how we can design intercultural training programs to enable people ﾠto become competent intercultural communicators. With knowledge, skills, and application of knowledge and skills in intergroup and intercultural contexts, this course builds your capacity to interactﾠ effectively and appropriately ﾠwith individuals, groups, and communities in local, national and international contexts.

UQ Public Timetable
.
Additional timetable information
Whilst every effort is made to place students in their preferred activity, it is not always possible for a student to be enrolled in their tutorial of choice. If you require assistance, please ensure that you email
timetabling.commarts@enquire.uq.edu.au
from your
UQ student email
with:
· Your name
· Your student ID
· The course code
· A list of three tutorial preferences (in order of preference)
· Reason for the change – e.g. timetable clash, elite athlete status, SAP
Teaching staff do not have access to change tutorials or help with timetables; all timetabling changes must be processed through the Timetabling Team.
course_aims: This course aims to provide you with advanced and integrated knowledge of intercultural communication theories and skills thatﾠyou can apply inﾠprofessional practice. The course critically analyses the key role communication and culture play in how you perceiveﾠyourselfﾠand others culturally, developeﾠintercultural and intergroup relations, manageﾠconflicts, and buildﾠintercultural communication competence. This courseﾠequips you with the capacity toﾠapply knowledge and skills of intercultural communication to resolve problems in real-life situations as well as transmit knowledge and skills to specialist and non-specialist audiences.

learning_outcomes.description: Demonstrate specialised, advanced and integrated knowledge of intercultural communication theories and skills.
Analyse critically the key role that communication plays in developing intercultural relations and in managing conflicts.
Evaluate how media and culture intersect to influence attitudes, perceptions and public opinions as well as cultural change.
Transmit knowledge and skills to specialist and non-specialist audiences.
Communicate effectively and appropriately with individuals, groups, and communities in diverse local, national and cultural contexts.

assessment_details.title: Reflective Essay
assessment_details.task_description: Assessment 1 (Reflective Essay) requires you to reflect on your own culture as an "insider". Your cultural group can be defined at a national level (e.g., Korean, Australian, or German) or at a subculture level (e.g., religious groups). In your reflective essay, you should first state your cultural identification, position yourself as a member of the cultural group you identify with, be it national or a subcultural group. Then you explain how your cultural identification conveys a sense of identity to you; what beliefs, values, practices, traditions and so forth are considered important and why they are important to you and your cultural group. You may include examples of products or practices of your culture but your reflective essay should go beyond simply introducing or describing what the cultural products (e.g., a type of traditional food) or practices (e.g., eating certain traditional food on a special occasion) are. You should explain the cultural meanings of those products or practices and what they tell us about the cultural beliefs and values important to your cultural group and how those beliefs and values influence your thinking (attitude), doing (behaviour) and being (identity). Reflecting on your own culture will help you to appreciate differences as well as similarities between cultures, which prepares you for Assessment 3. You need to apply at least one theoretical concept or a theory learned in the course in relation to culture, identity, and communication. Define all theoretical concepts you choose to use in your reflective essay to demonstrate your knowledge of the concepts and your ability to apply them. You may cite academic and non-academic references for your reflective essay. Do not cite Wikipedia as academic references for your essay. Include all references cited within your essay in the references list at the end. APA 7th edition referencing style is required. The UQ Library website has APA 7th edition referencing style guide. The word limit (1500) for this assessment is suggestive. No penalties will apply based on the sole reason that your assessment is over/under the suggested word limit. Further information on this assessment will be provided in class sessions and on Blackboard. Include the following sections in your reflective essay: Title of your reflective essay. Brief description of the cultural group(s) with which you identify. The theoretical concept(s) and/or theories you choose to frame the discussion of your culture. Your reflection on how your culture shapes your attitude and behaviour. References list: All references cited within the essay should be included in the references list. Note: This assessment should be submitted via Turnitin. Submission to the course coordinator or your tutor as email attachment will NOT be accepted. You are not required to include pictures in your reflective essay. However, if you choose to include pictures, please limit your pictures to maximum 2. You might experience problems submitting your assessment via Turnitin if your file size is too large. Your assessment is evaluated according to the marking criteria in this course, NOT evaluated with reference to the achievement of other students or your performance in other courses. This task has been designed to be challenging, authentic and complex. Whilst students may use AI and/or MT technologies, successful completion of assessment in this course will require students to critically engage in specific contexts and tasks for which artificial intelligence will provide only limited support and guidance. A failure to reference generative AI or MT use may constitute student misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct. Read full task description Hide full task description
assessment_details.submission_guidelines:
Submit via TurnItIn on Blackboard.
TurnItIn Receipts:
This assignment will be submitted electronically via Blackboard and using TurnItIn . Before submitting any assignments for this course, you must ensure you have completed UQ's compulsory online Academic Integrity Tutorial.
When you successfully submit your assessment to TurnItIn you will see text confirming your submission is complete, before being redirected to your Assignment inbox. On this page you can:
· View the name of the submitted file
· View date and time of the upload
· Resubmit your paper (if necessary)
· Download your submitted paper
· Download digital receipt.
If you cannot see your submission in your Assignment inbox you should regard your submission as unsuccessful. Students are responsible for retaining evidence of submission by the due date for all assessment items, in the required form (e.g. screenshot, email, photo, and an unaltered copy of submitted work).
If the submission was not successful:
· Note the error message (preferably take a screenshot).
· Go to your assignment page and see if it is possible to submit again.
· If you cannot submit again email your course coordinator immediately with the assignment attached.
Please visit this webpage for further advice on how to submit your TurnItIn assignment
Deferral or extension
You may be able to apply for an extension .
The maximum extension allowed is 28 days. Extensions are given in multiples of 24 hours.
Late submission
A penalty of 10% of the maximum possible mark will be deducted per 24 hours from time submission is due for up to 7 days. After 7 days, you will receive a mark of 0.

assessment_details.title: In-Class Tests (5 tests)
assessment_details.task_description: Assessment 2 consists of 5 in-class tests to be conducted in person during tutorial sessions over 5 weeks, starting in Week 4 and ending in Week 9 (inclusive). No in-class test is scheduled in Week 6 because Friday of Week 6 is a public holiday. Each in-class test weighs 6 marks. The duration of each test is about 30 minutes. Electronic devices, readings, and written notes are not permitted during the test. Tutors will provide hard copies of test worksheets to students in their tutorial session of the week, and students submit their completed test worksheet to their tutor at the end of the test time in the tutorial session. In--class tests are in the format of short-answer questions. The question(s) of each test are based on the learning materials of a specific week including lecture notes and power points, tutorial notes and power points, and required readings that are available on Blackboard. More details of in-class tests will be provided in class sessions and on Blackboard course site. Note: Each completed in-class test worksheet is to be submitted in the tutorial session in which the test is conducted. Submission of a completed in-class test worksheet to the course coordinator or tutor as email attachment will NOT be accepted. Your assessment is evaluated according to the marking criteria specified in this course, NOT evaluated with reference to the achievement of other students or your performance in other courses. This assessment task is to be completed in-person. The use of generative Artificial Intelligence (AI) or Machine Translation (MT) tools will not be permitted. Any attempted use of AI or MT may constitute student misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct. Read full task description Hide full task description
assessment_details.submission_guidelines:
Submit in tutorial sessions.
Each completed in-class test worksheet is to be submitted in the tutorial session in which the test is conducted. Submission of a completed in-class test worksheet to the course coordinator or tutor as email attachment will NOT be accepted.
Deferral or extension
You cannot defer or apply for an extension for this assessment.
Extension or deferral for this assessment is not available because each in-class test is conducted and completed in a tutorial session. If you are unable to take an in-class test in a specific week due to illness or exceptional circumstances, please contact your tutor or the course coordinator via email and attach your supporting documents. If your application for taking the make-up test is approved, your tutor will arrange a time and venue for you to take the make-up test.
Late submission
You will receive a mark of 0 if this assessment is submitted late .
No late submission is permitted as this is in-class and in-person assessment. Please refer to the Extension or Deferral availability notes below if you are unable to take an in-class test on the day of your tutorial in a specific week.

assessment_details.title: Intercultural Experience Report
assessment_details.task_description: Assessment 3 (Intercultural Experience Report) requires you to report on your experience of communicating with people from another culture as an "outsider". To accomplish this assessment task, you need to choose a culture or subculture other than your own, i.e. you are an "outsider" to this culture. You then need to search for academic and non-academic sources to learn about this culture and cultural group. Based on your research, you identify some aspects of the culture (e.g., certain cultural practices, traditions, values, beliefs, etc.) that you want to learn more about and provide a justification for why you choose to learn more about them. For the interaction part, locate about three persons from the cultural group you have chosen through personal, social, or academic networks (e.g., friends, colleagues, classmates, or professional associations of which you are a member, etc.). Interview them via a suitable medium (in person or online) to learn about their views regarding those aspects of the culture you are interested in learning more about. In other words, you learn about the culture you have chosen to study through interacting with people who are insiders of that culture. Provide your interview questions and summarise your main findings from interviews. Identify one or a set of potential intercultural problems that might arise in communication between members of the cultural group under study and members of your own culture (e.g., stereotypes, different beliefs and values, communication styles, etc.). Apply at least one theory to analyse the identified intercultural problem(s) and provide specific possible solutions, not just general statements. In the reflection part of your intercultural experience report, discuss your learning from this intercultural communication experience. You may cite academic and non-academic references for your report. Do not cite Wikipedia as academic references for your essay. Include all references cited within your report in the references list at the end. APA 7th edition referencing style is required. The UQ Library website has APA 7th edition referencing style guide. The word limit (2000) for this assessment is suggestive. No penalties will apply based on the sole reason that your assessment is over/under the word limit. Further information on this assessment will be provided in class sessions and on Blackboard. Include the following sections in your report: Title of your intercultural report. Introduction: State your own cultural identity in the Introduction and the culture you have chosen to study as well as provide a justification for your choice of the culture to study as an outsider. Description of the culture under study: Provide a brief description of the specific aspects of culture you plan to focus on. Intercultural interaction: Construct 3 to 5 interview questions you plan to ask to the members of the cultural group you have chosen to study and provide the interview questions in your assignment. Describe the characteristics of the persons you have chosen to interview and explain why you believe they are in the best position to answer your questions. Then provide a summary of your main findings from your interviews. There is no need to include the real names of the persons you have interviewed. Problem statement and application of theories: Identify one or a set of potential intercultural problems that might arise in communication between people from the cultural group under study and people from your own cultural group; analyse the problem(s) and provide specific possible solutions by drawing on theories and concepts learned in the course and the relevant literature you have searched. Reflection: Reflect on your experience of interacting with people from a cultural group different from your own and discuss what you have learned from this intercultural communication experience. References list: All references cited within the report should be included in the references list. APA 7th edition referencing style is required. The UQ Library website has APA 7th edition referencing style guide. Note: This assessment should be submitted via Turnitin. Submission to the course coordinator or tutor as email attachment will NOT be accepted. You are not required to include pictures in your report. However, if you choose to include pictures, please limit your pictures to maximum 2. You might experience problems submitting your assessment via Turnitin if your file size is too large. Your assessment is evaluated according to the marking criteria in this course, NOT evaluated with reference to the achievement of other students or your performance in other courses. This task has been designed to be challenging, authentic and complex. Whilst students may use AI and/or MT technologies, successful completion of assessment in this course will require students to critically engage in specific contexts and tasks for which artificial intelligence will provide only limited support and guidance. A failure to reference generative AI or MT use may constitute student misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct. Read full task description Hide full task description

learning_activities.topic: Week 1 Lecture: Introduction to the Course This first lecture provides an overview of the course structure, learning activities, assessments, requirements, and other course related information. Learning outcomes: L01
Week 1 Tutorial: No tutorial Tutorials commence in Week 2.
Week 2 Lecture: Culture and People This lecture discusses dynamics of culture and explains how culture influences our thinking, doing and being. Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L05
Week 2 Tutorial: Culture and behaviour This tutorial discusses applications of theories to study and analyse culture. Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L05
Week 3 Lecture: Communication and Culture This lecture explores models of communication and various ways in which communication reflects culture. Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03
Week 3 Tutorial: Communicating Culture This tutorial discusses ways in which communication and culture mutually influence each other. Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03
Week 4 Lecture: Social Categorisation and Perception This lecture explores theories and processes of social categorisation and perception. Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03, L05

Week 4 Tutorial: Perception and Culture This tutorial discusses the influence of culture on how we view ourselves and others who are from our cultural group or who are not from our cultural group. Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03, L05
Week 5 Lecture: Building Human Relationships This lecture examines theories of human relationships and the development of intercultural relationships. Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03, L04, L05

Week 5 Tutorial: Intergroup and Intercultural Relations This tutorial analyses cultural influences on developing and maintaining intercultural and intergroup relations. Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03, L04, L05
Week 6 Lecture: Media and Cultural Change This lecture explores ways in which various types of media bring about change in society, culture, and people. Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03, L04, L05
Week 6 Tutorial: NO TUTORIALS THIS WEEK No tutorials this week because Friday is a public holiday.
Mid-Semester Break Mid-Semester break. No lecture and no tutorials. Enjoy your mid-semester break!

Week 7 Lecture: Immigration and Acculturation This lecture discusses immigration as a key source of cultural diversity and various strategies of acculturation. Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03, L04, L05

Week 7 Tutorial: Integration and Identity Negotiation This tutorial applies theories of acculturation to analyse cross-cultural adjustment experiences of immigrants and refugees. Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03, L04, L05
Week 8 Lecture: Diversity in Workplaces This lecture explores how orientations to cultural dimensions influence behaviour in workplaces. Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03, L04, L05

Week 8 Tutorial: Business and Culture This tutorial applies theories of cultural dimensions to analyse the influence of culture on attitudes and behavious in workplaces. Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03, L04, L05
Week 9 Lecture: Working in Culturally Diverse Communities This lecture discusses various challenges as well as benefits of working in diverse communities. Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03, L04, L05

Week 9 Tutorial: Engaging with communities This tutorial discusses culturally responsive communication strategies when engaging with culturally diverse communities. Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03, L04, L05
Week 10 Lecture: Self-Directed Learning This week is self-directed learning. No lecture. Monday 4 May is Labour Day Public Holiday. Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03, L04, L05

Week 10 Tutorial: Self-Directed Learning This week is self-directed learning. No tutorials. You may use the time to get started with working on your Assessment 3. Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03, L04, L05
Week 11 Lecture: Intercultural Communication in Families This lecture discusses intercultural and intergenerational communication in family contexts. Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03, L04, L05
Week 11 Tutorial: Intercultural Families This tutorial analyses communication in intercultural and inter-racial families. Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03, L04, L05

Week 12 Lecture: Intercultural Competence Training This lecture discusses how to design training programs to build intercultural competence in people working and living in diverse cultural contexts. Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03, L04, L05
Week 12 Tutorial: Questions and Answers This final tutorial is a drop-in session for questions and answers about Assessment 3. Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03, L04, L05
Week 13 Lecture: Self-Directed Learning This week is self-directed learning. No lecture. You may use this time to work on your Assessment 3. Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03, L04, L05
Week 13 Tutorial: Self-Directed Learning This week is self-directed learning. No tutorials. You may use this time to work on your Assessment 3. Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03, L04, L05

policies_and_procedures: University policies and procedures apply to all aspects of student life. As a UQ student, you must comply with University-wide and program-specific requirements, including the: Student Code of Conduct Policy Student Integrity and Misconduct Policy and Procedure Assessment Procedure Examinations Procedure Reasonable Adjustments for Students Policy and Procedure AI for Assessment Guide Recording of Teaching Policy and Procedure Learn more about UQ policies on my.UQ and the Policy and Procedure Library . Course guidelines Communication Expectations While you are a student at UQ, all communication must be conducted according to the UQ Student Code of Conduct . The UQ Library has a helpful Communicate and collaborate online module . Email is the primary way for you to send messages to, and receive information from, the School and our staff. You must use your UQ email address (not a private address) to communicate with staff. You should add a clear subject line, including course code, and a 2-3 word statement. You can send email at any time, however please do not expect responses outside normal working hours (Monday to Friday from ~8am to ~5pm). Emails that constitute bullying, harassment or discrimination against staff contravene the Student Code of Conduct. Emails like this will be reported to the University, and the matter will be pursued as misconduct. Learning activities
